campaigns.title: Suspected China-Based Espionage Operation Against Military Targets in Southeast Asia
campaigns.analyst_summary: CL-STA-1087 deployed AppleChris backdoor using DLL side-loading (T1574.002) to establish persistence via Windows services (T1543.003) and maintain command-and-control through PowerShell-based reverse shells. The malware, identified by its distinctive mutex "0XFEXYCDAPPLE05CHRIS," communicates with multiple C2 infrastructure nodes including 154.39.142.177 and 8.212.169.27 using web protocols. The campaign leverages PowerShell execution (T1059.001) and service-based execution (T1569.002) for initial compromise and maintaining access to victim environments.

iocs.context: The PowerShell scripts were designed to create reverse shells to one of four command and control (C2) servers.
The PowerShell scripts were designed to create reverse shells to one of four command and control (C2) servers.
The PowerShell scripts were designed to create reverse shells to one of four command and control (C2) servers.
The PowerShell scripts were designed to create reverse shells to one of four command and control (C2) servers.
We named this backdoor AppleChris, after the 0XFEXYCDAPPLE05CHRIS mutex that forms part of the malware infection chain.
The initial iteration represents the earlier development phase, with the filename swrpv.sys for the Dropbox variant of AppleChris.
The more recent Tunneler variant with expanded capabilities uses the name update.exe.
MemFun is multi-stage malware that consists of three components including an initial loader named GoogleUpdate.exe.
The attackers performed DLL hijacking by placing the malicious swprv32.sys AppleChris DLL in the system32 directory.
Example domain used in IoC triage training table as a suspicious domain for educational purposes.
Example IP address used in IoC handling table and log hunting training, described as attacker server or C2 beacon.
Example URL for malicious file download link used in IoC classification training.
IP address appearing in phishing email header example in Received header from mailgw1.example.net.
IP address in phishing email header example appearing in Received header from unknown HELO laptop.

Suspicious Reply-To and Return-Path address in phishing email header training example.

ttps.description: Attackers executed malicious PowerShell scripts remotely across selected systems to create reverse shells to C2 servers
ttps.evidence: Cortex XDR agents, newly deployed across the environment, detected suspicious PowerShell activity indicating an existing compromise. The detection revealed an ongoing attack targeting multiple endpoints within the network. Attackers established persistence on an unmanaged endpoint that they used to execute malicious PowerShell scripts remotely across selected systems.

ttps.description: Malware used HTTP GET requests with custom HTTP verbs for C2 communications
ttps.evidence: Each command response utilizes custom HTTP requests as communication parameters (PUT, POT, DPF, UPF, CPF, LPF) to facilitate command tracking and response handling.

ttps.description: DLL hijacking allowed malware to leverage elevated privileges through Volume Shadow Copy Service
ttps.evidence: The attackers performed DLL hijacking by placing the malicious swprv32.sys AppleChris DLL in the system32 directory. Subsequently, they established persistence by registering the malicious DLL as a component of the Volume Shadow Copy Service. This allowed the malware to leverage elevated privileges while masquerading as a legitimate Windows process to evade detection.

ttps.description: Attackers created new service to facilitate payload execution
ttps.evidence: To establish persistence, the attackers created a new service to facilitate payload execution.

ttps.description: Attackers created new service to establish persistence and facilitate payload execution
ttps.evidence: To establish persistence, the attackers created a new service to facilitate payload execution.

ttps.description: Attackers used WMI to deploy malware to additional endpoints across the network
ttps.evidence: From this initial foothold, the attackers orchestrated a systematic spread across the network. They used a combination of Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) and native Windows .NET commands to deploy malware to additional endpoints.

ttps.description: Attackers orchestrated systematic spread across network using WMI and .NET commands to deploy malware
ttps.evidence: From this initial foothold, the attackers orchestrated a systematic spread across the network. They used a combination of Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) and native Windows .NET commands to deploy malware to additional endpoints.

ttps.description: C2 IP address retrieved from Pastebin underwent Base64 decoding and RSA-1024 decryption
ttps.evidence: The retrieved content undergoes a two-stage decryption process: The raw text is Base64-decoded. The decoded text is decrypted using an embedded RSA-1024 private key.

ttps.description: Server response payload was decrypted using AES with session ID-based key
ttps.evidence: The server's response contains the command payload, which is then decrypted using AES. The 10-byte session ID, padded with 14 zeros, serves as the key.

ttps.description: Registration data was RSA-encrypted before transmission to C2 server
ttps.evidence: The malware generates a 10-byte random sequence as a unique session identifier, which is concatenated with the computer name and hex-encoded MAC address. This registration data is RSA-encrypted and transmitted to the C2 server within the payload of an HTTP GET request.

ttps.description: Malware variants employed sleep timers of 30-120 seconds to outlast automated sandbox monitoring windows
ttps.evidence: To bypass automated security systems, some of the malware variants employ sandbox evasion tactics at runtime. These variants trigger delayed execution through sleep timers of 30 seconds (EXE) and 120 seconds (DLL), effectively outlasting the typical monitoring windows of automated sandboxes.

ttps.description: Retrieved Pastebin content was Base64-decoded and RSA-decrypted to obtain C2 IP
ttps.evidence: The backdoor accesses a specific Pastebin URL to retrieve the encrypted C2 IP address. The retrieved content undergoes a two-stage decryption process: The raw text is Base64-decoded. The decoded text is decrypted using an embedded RSA-1024 private key.

ttps.description: Backdoors used Pastebin and Dropbox accounts as dead drop resolvers to dynamically resolve C2 server IP addresses
ttps.evidence: Both backdoors use the same Pastebin repository to resolve their respective C2 addresses. AppleChris employs a DDR technique to dynamically resolve its C2 server IP address. The Dropbox variant implements a dual DDR approach: Using an attacker-controlled Dropbox account as the primary DDR source, Falling back to a Pastebin-based DDR as a secondary option.

ttps.description: Malware implements command dispatcher with process enumeration capability
ttps.evidence: The malware implements a comprehensive command dispatcher that interprets single-byte command identifiers to execute a wide range of backdoor functionality, including: Process enumeration.

ttps.description: Attackers conducted selective searches for sensitive files related to military operations and organizational structures
ttps.evidence: After moving laterally through the network and establishing persistence, the attackers began to collect data. We observed highly selective searches for sensitive files related to: Official meeting records, Joint military activities, Detailed assessments of operational capabilities. The attackers showed particular interest in files related to military organizational structures and strategy.

ttps.description: Malware command dispatcher includes drive enumeration and directory listing capabilities
ttps.evidence: The malware implements a comprehensive command dispatcher that interprets single-byte command identifiers to execute a wide range of backdoor functionality, including: Drive enumeration, Directory listing.

ttps.description: Malware supports file upload and download commands; MemFun downloads additional DLL payload from C2 server
ttps.evidence: The malware implements a comprehensive command dispatcher that interprets single-byte command identifiers to execute a wide range of backdoor functionality, including: File upload, download and deletion. After the initial dropper execution, the entire attack chain operates in memory, employing evasion techniques and reflective loading. The loader's primary purpose is to establish communication with the C2 server and download an additional DLL.

ttps.description: Malware supports remote shell execution capability
ttps.evidence: The malware implements a comprehensive command dispatcher that interprets single-byte command identifiers to execute a wide range of backdoor functionality, including: Remote shell execution.

ttps.description: MemFun attack chain operates in memory employing reflective loading techniques
ttps.evidence: After the initial dropper execution, the entire attack chain operates in memory, employing evasion techniques and reflective loading.

ttps.description: PowerShell script content was decoded before execution
ttps.evidence: The decoded PowerShell script that was passed as a command-line argument.

ttps.description: Malware supports silent process creation capability
ttps.evidence: The malware implements a comprehensive command dispatcher that interprets single-byte command identifiers to execute a wide range of backdoor functionality, including: Silent process creation.

ttps.description: Tunneler variant supports command to activate proxy tunneling module
ttps.evidence: In addition, the Tunneler variant supports a command to activate the proxy tunneling module.

ttps.description: Malware includes file deletion capability in command dispatcher
ttps.evidence: The malware implements a comprehensive command dispatcher that interprets single-byte command identifiers to execute a wide range of backdoor functionality, including: File upload, download and deletion.

ttps.evidence: utilizing techniques like DLL hijacking and process hollowing to evade detection

sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects when PowerShell creates reverse shell connections to known AppleChris C2 servers, which is how CL-STA-1087 maintains remote access to compromised military targets.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: AppleChris PowerShell Reverse Shell to Known C2 Servers
id: rule-0001
status: experimental
description: Detects PowerShell connections to known AppleChris C2 infrastructure used by CL-STA-1087 for remote command execution
logsource:
  category: network_connection
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\powershell.exe'
    DestinationIp:
      - '154.39.142.177'
      - '154.39.137.203'
      - '8.212.169.27'
      - '109.248.24.177'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Unlikely, these IPs are confirmed malicious C2 infrastructure
level: critical

sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects creation of the unique AppleChris mutex value that the malware uses to prevent multiple instances from running simultaneously on infected systems.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: AppleChris Backdoor Mutex Creation
id: rule-0002
status: experimental
description: Detects creation of the distinctive 0XFEXYCDAPPLE05CHRIS mutex used by AppleChris malware to ensure single-instance execution
logsource:
  product: windows
  category: process_creation
detection:
  selection:
    CommandLine|contains: '0XFEXYCDAPPLE05CHRIS'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - None expected, this is a unique malware-specific mutex
level: critical

sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects DLL hijacking where AppleChris places malicious swprv32.sys in system32 to abuse Volume Shadow Copy Service for persistence with elevated privileges.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: AppleChris DLL Hijacking via Volume Shadow Copy Service
id: rule-0003
status: experimental
description: Detects AppleChris malware performing DLL side-loading by placing malicious swprv32.sys in system32 to hijack Volume Shadow Copy Service
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|endswith: '\system32\swprv32.sys'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate Volume Shadow Copy Service updates (verify file hash and signature)
level: high

sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects execution of suspicious GoogleUpdate.exe from non-standard locations, which is the initial MemFun loader component used in this espionage campaign.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: Suspicious GoogleUpdate.exe Execution from Non-Standard Location
id: rule-0004
status: experimental
description: Detects execution of GoogleUpdate.exe from unusual paths, indicative of MemFun malware loader used by CL-STA-1087
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\GoogleUpdate.exe'
  filter:
    Image|startswith:
      - 'C:\Program Files (x86)\Google\Update\'
      - 'C:\Program Files\Google\Update\'
  condition: selection and not filter
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate Google Update installations in non-standard directories
level: medium

sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects WMI being used to execute PowerShell scripts across multiple endpoints, which is how CL-STA-1087 spreads AppleChris laterally through the network.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: WMI Remote PowerShell Execution for Lateral Movement
id: rule-0005
status: experimental
description: Detects use of WMI to remotely execute PowerShell, matching CL-STA-1087 lateral movement technique to spread AppleChris malware
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    ParentImage|endswith: '\wmiprvse.exe'
    Image|endswith: '\powershell.exe'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate system administration using WMI and PowerShell remoting
  - Software deployment systems
level: high

snort_rules.plain_english: Detects outbound HTTP connections to the four known AppleChris C2 servers used for reverse shell command and control communications.
snort_rules.rule: alert tcp $HOME_NET any -> [154.39.142.177,154.39.137.203,8.212.169.27,109.248.24.177] any (msg:"MALWARE AppleChris C2 Communication to Known Server"; flow:established,to_server; sid:9200001; rev:1; classtype:trojan-activity; priority:1; metadata:malware AppleChris, actor CL-STA-1087;)

snort_rules.plain_english: Detects AppleChris malware using custom HTTP verbs like PUT, POT, DPF, UPF, CPF, or LPF which are unique to its command tracking and response mechanism.
snort_rules.rule: alert tcp $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET $HTTP_PORTS (msg:"MALWARE AppleChris Custom HTTP Verb C2 Communication"; flow:established,to_server; content:"POT "; depth:4; content:"DPF "; depth:4; content:"UPF "; depth:4; content:"CPF "; depth:4; content:"LPF "; depth:4; fast_pattern; sid:9200002; rev:1; classtype:trojan-activity; priority:1; metadata:malware AppleChris, actor CL-STA-1087;)

snort_rules.plain_english: Detects HTTP GET requests containing Base64-encoded and RSA-encrypted registration data with MAC address patterns used during AppleChris initial C2 beacon.
snort_rules.rule: alert tcp $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET $HTTP_PORTS (msg:"MALWARE AppleChris Registration Beacon with Encrypted MAC Address"; flow:established,to_server; content:"GET"; http_method; content:"/"; http_uri; pcre:"/[A-Za-z0-9+\/]{100,}={0,2}/U"; sid:9200003; rev:1; classtype:trojan-activity; priority:1; metadata:malware AppleChris, actor CL-STA-1087;)

snort_rules.plain_english: Detects PowerShell reverse shell connections on common ports to the four known AppleChris C2 infrastructure servers used for remote command execution.
snort_rules.rule: alert tcp $HOME_NET any -> [154.39.142.177,154.39.137.203,8.212.169.27,109.248.24.177] [443,4444,8080,8443] (msg:"MALWARE AppleChris PowerShell Reverse Shell Connection"; flow:established,to_server; content:"powershell"; nocase; sid:9200004; rev:1; classtype:trojan-activity; priority:1; metadata:malware AppleChris, actor CL-STA-1087;)

snort_rules.plain_english: Detects HTTP responses containing AES-encrypted payloads with 10-byte session identifiers used by AppleChris to deliver commands from the C2 server.
snort_rules.rule: alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET $HTTP_PORTS -> $HOME_NET any (msg:"MALWARE AppleChris AES Encrypted Command Response from C2"; flow:established,from_server; content:"200"; http_stat_code; content:"|0d 0a|"; content:"|0d 0a|"; within:4; isdataat:10,relative; sid:9200005; rev:1; classtype:trojan-activity; priority:1; metadata:malware AppleChris, actor CL-STA-1087;)

splunk.name: AppleChris C2 Network Connections
splunk.description: Detects network connections to known AppleChris C2 infrastructure IPs

splunk.name: AppleChris Malicious File Activity
splunk.description: Hunts for AppleChris-related suspicious filenames including DLL hijacking attempts
splunk.query: index=* (file_name IN ("swrpv.sys", "swprv32.sys", "update.exe", "GoogleUpdate.exe") OR file_path IN ("*\\system32\\swprv32.sys", "*\\swrpv.sys", "*\\GoogleUpdate.exe")) | stats count by file_name, file_path, file_hash, process_name, user, dest

splunk.name: PowerShell Reverse Shell Creation
splunk.description: Detects suspicious PowerShell execution with network connections indicating reverse shell activity
splunk.query: index=* sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security OR sourcetype=Powershell EventCode IN (4688, 4104, 4103) (process_name="powershell.exe" OR process_name="pwsh.exe") (CommandLine="*Net.Sockets*" OR CommandLine="*TCPClient*" OR CommandLine="*GetStream*" OR CommandLine="*Invoke-Expression*" OR CommandLine="*IEX*") | stats count by Computer, user, process_name, CommandLine, parent_process_name

kql.name: AppleChris C2 Communications
kql.description: Identifies network connections to known AppleChris C2 servers from endpoints
kql.query: DeviceNetworkEvents | where RemoteIP in ("154.39.142.177", "154.39.137.203", "8.212.169.27", "109.248.24.177") or InitiatingProcessFileName has_any ("powershell.exe", "pwsh.exe", "GoogleUpdate.exe", "update.exe") | project Timestamp, DeviceName, InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessCommandLine, RemoteIP, RemotePort, RemoteUrl, LocalIP, ActionType | order by Timestamp desc

kql.name: AppleChris DLL Hijacking and Persistence
kql.description: Detects AppleChris malware filenames and DLL side-loading into system32 directory
kql.query: DeviceFileEvents | where FileName in~ ("swrpv.sys", "swprv32.sys", "update.exe", "GoogleUpdate.exe") or (FolderPath has "system32" and FileName endswith ".sys" and FileName in~ ("swprv32.sys", "swrpv.sys")) or InitiatingProcessCommandLine has_any ("vssvc.exe", "Volume Shadow Copy") | project Timestamp, DeviceName, FileName, FolderPath, SHA256, InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessCommandLine, FileOriginUrl | order by Timestamp desc

kql.name: WMI and PowerShell Lateral Movement
kql.description: Hunts for lateral movement using WMI and PowerShell with potential reverse shell characteristics
kql.query: DeviceProcessEvents | where (FileName =~ "powershell.exe" or FileName =~ "pwsh.exe") and (ProcessCommandLine has_any ("Net.Sockets", "TCPClient", "GetStream", "Invoke-Expression", "IEX", "-e ", "-enc", "FromBase64String") or InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "wmiprvse.exe" or InitiatingProcessFileName =~ "WmiPrvSE.exe") | project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, FileName, ProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessParentFileName | order by Timestamp desc

eql.name: AppleChris Service Creation and DLL Hijacking Chain
eql.description: Detects service creation followed by malicious DLL loading into system32 for persistence
eql.query: sequence by ComputerName with maxspan=5m [process where event_id == 1 and process_name in ("sc.exe", "net.exe") and process_cmd_line like "*create*"] [file where event_id == 11 and file_path like "*\\system32\\*" and file_name in ("swprv32.sys", "swrpv.sys", "update.exe", "GoogleUpdate.exe")]

eql.name: PowerShell Reverse Shell to Known C2
eql.description: Identifies PowerShell execution with network connection to AppleChris C2 infrastructure
eql.query: sequence by ComputerName with maxspan=2m [process where event_id == 1 and process_name in ("powershell.exe", "pwsh.exe") and (process_cmd_line like "*Net.Sockets*" or process_cmd_line like "*TCPClient*" or process_cmd_line like "*GetStream*")] [network where event_id == 3 and (DestinationIp == "154.39.142.177" or DestinationIp == "154.39.137.203" or DestinationIp == "8.212.169.27" or DestinationIp == "109.248.24.177")]

eql.name: WMI Lateral Movement with Malware Deployment
eql.description: Detects WMI usage for lateral movement followed by AppleChris malware file creation
eql.query: sequence by ComputerName with maxspan=10m [process where event_id == 1 and process_name == "WmiPrvSE.exe" and parent_process_name in ("svchost.exe", "wmiprvse.exe")] [file where event_id == 11 and file_name in ("swrpv.sys", "swprv32.sys", "update.exe", "GoogleUpdate.exe")]

adversary.name: CL-STA-1087 AppleChris Campaign
adversary.description: This simulation profile emulates CL-STA-1087's AppleChris malware campaign targeting military organizations. The threat actor deployed DLL side-loading techniques through Volume Shadow Copy Service hijacking, established persistence via service creation, and used encrypted C2 communications with custom HTTP verbs. Attackers leveraged WMI and PowerShell for lateral movement and remote execution across compromised networks.

abilities.name: Simulate PowerShell Reverse Shell Execution
abilities.plain_english: Tests whether your EDR detects suspicious PowerShell network connection attempts that mimic reverse shell behavior - the technique CL-STA-1087 used to remotely execute malicious scripts across compromised endpoints.
abilities.ability_yaml:
- id: 8f7c3a1b-9e2d-4c5a-b6f8-1d3e7a9c4b2f
  name: Simulate PowerShell Reverse Shell Execution
  description: Simulates AppleChris malware using PowerShell for remote execution and C2 communication
  tactic: execution
  technique:
    attack_id: T1059.001
    name: PowerShell
  platforms:
    windows:
      psh:
        command: |
          Write-Host "[SIM] AppleChris PowerShell execution simulation"
          $simScript = "$env:TEMP\applechris_sim.ps1"
          $content = @'
          # [SIM] Simulated reverse shell behavior
          Write-Host "[SIM] Establishing simulated C2 connection"
          $simC2 = "sim-c2-server.local"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Connected to: $simC2"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Session ID: SIM-$(Get-Random -Maximum 99999)"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Awaiting commands from C2"
          '@
          Set-Content -Path $simScript -Value $content
          powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File $simScript
          Write-Host "[SIM] PowerShell execution completed"
        cleanup: |
          Remove-Item -Path "$env:TEMP\applechris_sim.ps1" -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
          Write-Host "[SIM] Cleanup: Removed simulated PowerShell script"

abilities.name: Simulate DLL Side-Loading via VSS Hijack
abilities.plain_english: Tests whether security tools detect malicious DLL placement in system32 masquerading as a legitimate Windows component - the DLL hijacking technique AppleChris used to abuse Volume Shadow Copy Service for privilege escalation.
abilities.ability_yaml:
- id: 2d4e6f8a-1c3b-5a7d-9e2f-4b6c8a1d3e5f
  name: Simulate DLL Side-Loading via VSS Hijack
  description: Simulates AppleChris DLL hijacking of Volume Shadow Copy Service (swprv32.sys)
  tactic: privilege-escalation
  technique:
    attack_id: T1574.002
    name: DLL Side-Loading
  platforms:
    windows:
      psh:
        command: |
          Write-Host "[SIM] AppleChris DLL side-loading simulation"
          $simDLL = "$env:TEMP\swprv32_sim.dll"
          $simContent = "[SIM] This simulates the malicious AppleChris DLL (swprv32.sys) that hijacks VSS"
          Set-Content -Path $simDLL -Value $simContent
          Write-Host "[SIM] Created simulated malicious DLL: $simDLL"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Real attack would place in: C:\Windows\System32\swprv32.sys"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Target service: Volume Shadow Copy Service (swprv)"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Technique: DLL hijacking for privilege escalation"
          Get-Item $simDLL | Select-Object Name, Length, CreationTime
        cleanup: |
          Remove-Item -Path "$env:TEMP\swprv32_sim.dll" -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
          Write-Host "[SIM] Cleanup: Removed simulated DLL"

abilities.name: Simulate Malicious Service Creation for Persistence
abilities.plain_english: Tests whether your monitoring detects suspicious service creation with unusual binaries - the persistence mechanism CL-STA-1087 used to automatically execute AppleChris malware after system restarts.
abilities.ability_yaml:
- id: 5a7c9e1b-3d5f-7a9c-1e3b-5d7f9a1c3e5b
  name: Simulate Malicious Service Creation for Persistence
  description: Simulates AppleChris service-based persistence mechanism
  tactic: persistence
  technique:
    attack_id: T1543.003
    name: Windows Service
  platforms:
    windows:
      psh:
        command: |
          Write-Host "[SIM] AppleChris service persistence simulation"
          $serviceName = "AppleChrisSim"
          $simExe = "$env:TEMP\applechris_service_sim.exe"
          $simContent = "[SIM] Simulated AppleChris service executable"
          Set-Content -Path $simExe -Value $simContent
          Write-Host "[SIM] Created simulated service binary: $simExe"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Service name: $serviceName"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Real attack creates service targeting VSS component"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Service would execute: malicious DLL via service host"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Persistence: Service set to auto-start on boot"
          Write-Host "[SIM] NOTE: No actual service created in this simulation"
        cleanup: |
          Remove-Item -Path "$env:TEMP\applechris_service_sim.exe" -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
          Write-Host "[SIM] Cleanup: Removed simulated service binary"

abilities.name: Simulate WMI-Based Lateral Movement
abilities.plain_english: Tests whether security monitoring detects WMI being used to remotely execute commands on other systems - the lateral movement technique CL-STA-1087 employed to spread AppleChris malware across the network.
abilities.ability_yaml:
- id: 9c1e3b5d-7f9a-1c3e-5b7d-9f1a3c5e7b9d
  name: Simulate WMI-Based Lateral Movement
  description: Simulates AppleChris using WMI for network propagation and remote execution
  tactic: execution
  technique:
    attack_id: T1047
    name: Windows Management Instrumentation
  platforms:
    windows:
      psh:
        command: |
          Write-Host "[SIM] AppleChris WMI lateral movement simulation"
          $simTargets = @("SIM-HOST-001", "SIM-HOST-002", "SIM-HOST-003")
          $simPayload = "$env:TEMP\applechris_payload_sim.exe"
          Set-Content -Path $simPayload -Value "[SIM] AppleChris malware payload"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Created simulated payload: $simPayload"
          foreach ($target in $simTargets) {
            Write-Host "[SIM] Attempting WMI connection to: $target"
            Write-Host "[SIM] WMI Query: SELECT * FROM Win32_Process WHERE Name='explorer.exe'"
            Write-Host "[SIM] Would execute: wmic /node:$target process call create '$simPayload'"
            Write-Host "[SIM] Deployment status: Simulated success on $target"
          }
          Write-Host "[SIM] Lateral movement simulation complete"
        cleanup: |
          Remove-Item -Path "$env:TEMP\applechris_payload_sim.exe" -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
          Write-Host "[SIM] Cleanup: Removed simulated payload file"

abilities.name: Simulate Encrypted C2 with Custom HTTP Verbs
abilities.plain_english: Tests whether network monitoring detects unusual HTTP methods and encrypted payloads that mimic C2 traffic - the command and control technique AppleChris used with custom verbs like PUT, POT, DPF for stealth communications.
abilities.ability_yaml:
- id: 3e5b7d9f-1a3c-5e7b-9d1f-3a5c7e9b1d3f
  name: Simulate Encrypted C2 with Custom HTTP Verbs
  description: Simulates AppleChris C2 communications using custom HTTP verbs and encryption
  tactic: command-and-control
  technique:
    attack_id: T1071.001
    name: Web Protocols
  platforms:
    windows:
      psh:
        command: |
          Write-Host "[SIM] AppleChris encrypted C2 communication simulation"
          $c2Server = "sim-c2.pastebin-clone.local"
          $sessionID = -join ((0..9) | Get-Random -Count 10 | ForEach-Object {$_.ToString()})
          $computerName = $env:COMPUTERNAME
          $macAddress = "00-SIM-MAC-ADDR"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Generated Session ID: $sessionID"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Registration data: $computerName|$macAddress"
          Write-Host "[SIM] Simulating RSA-1024 encryption of registration data"
          $encryptedData = [Convert]::ToBase64String([Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes("$computerName|$macAddress"))
          Write-Host "[SIM] Base64-encoded (simulated encrypted): $encryptedData"
          $customVerbs = @("PUT", "POT", "DPF", "UPF", "CPF", "LPF")
          foreach ($verb in $customVerbs) {
            Write-Host "[SIM] HTTP $verb request to $c2Server with encrypted payload"
            Write-Host "[SIM] Response would be AES-decrypted using session ID as key"
          }
          Write-Host "[SIM] C2 communication complete"
        cleanup: |
          Write-Host "[SIM] Cleanup: No files created, simulation complete"

campaigns.title: Stryker attack wiped tens of thousands of devices, no malware needed
campaigns.analyst_summary: Handala threat actors deployed a custom wiper malware that performs MBR and file overwriting operations, destroying data through directory deletion and disk wiping techniques (T1485, T1561). The attackers used Active Directory reconnaissance via ADRecon and distributed the wiper through GPO logon scripts after establishing persistence through account manipulation (T1098) and cloud account compromise (T1078.004). The campaign demonstrates a destructive focus targeting enterprise networks with coordinated reconnaissance, lateral movement, and data destruction capabilities.

sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects execution of the ADRecon PowerShell reconnaissance script (dra.ps1) used by Handala to enumerate Active Directory environments before deploying their wiper malware.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: Handala ADRecon PowerShell Script Execution
id: rule-0001
status: experimental
description: Detects execution of dra.ps1, the ADRecon PowerShell-based reconnaissance framework used by Handala threat actor to enumerate Active Directory environments prior to destructive operations
logsource:
  product: windows
  category: process_creation
detection:
  selection:
    - CommandLine|contains: 'dra.ps1'
    - Image|endswith: '\powershell.exe'
      CommandLine|contains|all:
        - '.ps1'
        - 'ADRecon'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate use of ADRecon tool by authorized security teams
level: high

sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects execution of handala.bat batch file used by the Handala threat actor to distribute their custom wiper malware via GPO logon scripts and scheduled tasks across the network.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: Handala Wiper Distribution via Batch File
id: rule-0002
status: experimental
description: Detects execution of handala.bat batch file used to distribute Handala Wiper via GPO logon scripts and scheduled tasks
logsource:
  product: windows
  category: process_creation
detection:
  selection:
    - Image|endswith: '\cmd.exe'
      CommandLine|contains: 'handala.bat'
    - ParentImage|endswith:
        - '\svchost.exe'
        - '\taskeng.exe'
        - '\taskhostw.exe'
      CommandLine|contains: 'handala.bat'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Unlikely, filename is highly specific to this threat actor
level: critical

sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects creation of new Global Administrator accounts in Azure AD/Entra ID, which Handala uses to maintain persistent elevated access before executing mass device wipes via Intune.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: Suspicious Global Administrator Account Creation
id: rule-0003
status: experimental
description: Detects creation of new Global Administrator accounts in Azure AD, a persistence technique used by Handala before executing mass wipe operations via Intune
logsource:
  product: azure
  service: auditlogs
detection:
  selection:
    ActivityDisplayName: 'Add member to role'
    TargetResources.modifiedProperties.displayName: 'Role.DisplayName'
    TargetResources.modifiedProperties.newValue|contains: 'Global Administrator'
  timeframe: 1h
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate administrative role assignments during normal operations
  - Expected during new hire onboarding processes
level: high

sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects mass device wipe commands executed via Microsoft Intune, the exact technique Handala used to remotely destroy data on nearly 80,000 Stryker employee devices in March 2025.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: Mass Device Wipe via Microsoft Intune
id: rule-0004
status: experimental
description: Detects multiple device wipe commands executed via Microsoft Intune within a short timeframe, indicating potential destructive attack like Handala's mass wipe operation
logsource:
  product: azure
  service: auditlogs
detection:
  selection:
    ActivityDisplayName: 'Wipe device'
    Category: 'DeviceManagement'
  timeframe: 1h
  condition: selection | count() > 50
falsepositives:
  - Large-scale legitimate device retirement operations
  - Mass device refresh projects
level: critical

sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects VPN authentication attempts from commercial VPN providers and Starlink IP ranges that Handala uses to obfuscate their brute-force attacks against organizational VPN infrastructure.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: VPN Access from Handala-Associated IP Ranges
id: rule-0005
status: experimental
description: Detects VPN authentication attempts from commercial VPN nodes and Starlink IP ranges associated with Handala brute-force campaigns
logsource:
  product: windows
  service: security
detection:
  selection:
    EventID:
      - 4624
      - 4625
    LogonType: 3
    IpAddress|startswith:
      - '169.150.227.'
      - '149.88.26.'
      - '188.92.255.'
      - '209.198.131.'
      - '146.185.219.235'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate remote workers using VPN services
  - Employees traveling and using Starlink connectivity
level: medium

splunk.name: Handala Wiper Execution Detection
splunk.description: Detects execution of Handala wiper malware by MD5 hash and associated batch file distribution
splunk.query: index=* (EventCode=4688 OR sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security OR sourcetype=sysmon) (MD5="5986ab04dd6b3d259935249741d3eff2" OR MD5="3cb9dea916432ffb8784ac36d1f2d3cd" OR CommandLine="*handala.bat*" OR Image="*handala.bat*") | table _time host user CommandLine ParentImage MD5 | sort -_time

splunk.name: ADRecon PowerShell Reconnaissance Activity
splunk.description: Detects execution of ADRecon framework used by Handala for Active Directory enumeration
splunk.query: index=* (sourcetype=WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell/Operational OR EventCode=4104 OR EventCode=4688) (ScriptBlockText="*dra.ps1*" OR CommandLine="*dra.ps1*" OR OriginalFileName="dra.ps1") | table _time host user CommandLine ScriptBlockText | sort -_time

splunk.name: VPN Brute Force from Handala Infrastructure
splunk.description: Detects VPN authentication attempts from known Handala commercial VPN and Starlink IP ranges
splunk.query: index=* (sourcetype=vpn OR sourcetype=firewall OR EventCode=4625 OR EventCode=4624) (src_ip="169.150.227.*" OR src_ip="149.88.26.*" OR src_ip="188.92.255.*" OR src_ip="209.198.131.*" OR src_ip="146.185.219.235" OR src_ip="107.189.19.52") | stats count by _time src_ip user dest action | where count > 5 | sort -_time

kql.name: Handala Malware Hash Detection
kql.description: Identifies process or file events matching known Handala wiper MD5 hashes
kql.query: union DeviceProcessEvents, DeviceFileEvents | where MD5 in ('5986ab04dd6b3d259935249741d3eff2', '3cb9dea916432ffb8784ac36d1f2d3cd') or FileName has_any ('handala.bat', 'dra.ps1') | project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, FileName, FolderPath, ProcessCommandLine, MD5, InitiatingProcessCommandLine | sort by Timestamp desc

kql.name: Handala C2 Network Communication
kql.description: Detects network connections to known Handala command and control infrastructure
kql.query: DeviceNetworkEvents | where RemoteIP in ('107.189.19.52', '146.185.219.235') or RemoteIP startswith '169.150.227.' or RemoteIP startswith '149.88.26.' or RemoteIP startswith '188.92.255.' or RemoteIP startswith '209.198.131.' | project Timestamp, DeviceName, InitiatingProcessFileName, InitiatingProcessCommandLine, RemoteIP, RemotePort, RemoteUrl | sort by Timestamp desc

kql.name: PowerShell Deletion Script with Handala Characteristics
kql.description: Detects PowerShell execution deleting user directories consistent with Handala wiper activity
kql.query: DeviceProcessEvents | where FileName =~ 'powershell.exe' and (ProcessCommandLine has_all ('Remove-Item', 'Recurse', 'Force') or ProcessCommandLine has 'dra.ps1') | where ProcessCommandLine has_any ('Users', 'Documents', 'Desktop') or ProcessCommandLine has 'dra.ps1' | project Timestamp, DeviceName, AccountName, ProcessCommandLine, InitiatingProcessFileName, MD5 | sort by Timestamp desc

eql.name: Handala Wiper Distribution via GPO
eql.description: Detects sequence of batch file execution followed by wiper activity consistent with GPO-based distribution
eql.query: sequence by aid with maxspan=10m [process where event_simpleName="ProcessRollup2" and (FileName="handala.bat" or CommandLine="*handala.bat*")] [process where event_simpleName="ProcessRollup2" and (MD5HashData="5986ab04dd6b3d259935249741d3eff2" or MD5HashData="3cb9dea916432ffb8784ac36d1f2d3cd")]

eql.name: ADRecon Execution with Network Activity
eql.description: Correlates ADRecon PowerShell execution with subsequent network connections indicating data exfiltration
eql.query: sequence by aid with maxspan=30m [process where event_simpleName="ProcessRollup2" and FileName="powershell.exe" and CommandLine="*dra.ps1*"] [network where event_simpleName="NetworkConnectIP4" and (RemoteAddressIP4="107.189.19.52" or RemoteAddressIP4="146.185.219.235")]

eql.name: Brute Force VPN Access to Intune Wipe Activity
eql.description: Detects multiple failed authentication attempts followed by successful cloud admin activity consistent with Handala tactics
eql.query: sequence with maxspan=2h [authentication where event_simpleName="UserLogon" and LogonType_decimal="3" and success="false"] with runs=10 [authentication where event_simpleName="UserLogon" and success="true"] [process where event_simpleName="ProcessRollup2" and (CommandLine="*intune*" or CommandLine="*wipe*" or CommandLine="*devicemanagement*")]

campaigns.title: Arkanix Stealer: a C++ & Python infostealer
campaigns.analyst_summary: An unknown threat actor is distributing Arkanix Stealer through Python loader scripts disguised with social engineering lures like "discord_nitro_code_validator_right_aligned.py" that download the stealer payload from arkanix[.]pw infrastructure. sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects execution of Python scripts with suspicious filenames mimicking Discord Nitro, Steam, or TikTok tools that Arkanix uses to trick victims into running the loader stage.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: Arkanix Stealer Loader Script Execution
id: rule-0001
status: experimental
description: Detects execution of Python loader scripts used by Arkanix Stealer with social engineering filenames
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    - Image|endswith: '\python.exe'
      CommandLine|contains:
        - 'discord_nitro_code_validator_right_aligned.py'
        - 'steam_account_checker_pro_v1.py'
        - 'discord_nitro_checker.py'
    - OriginalFileName: 'TikTokAccountBotter.exe'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:

level: high

id: rule-0002
status: experimental
description: Detects file creation in Arkanix_lol staging directory used by the stealer

  selection:
    TargetFilename|contains: '\AppData\Local\Temp\upd_'
    TargetFilename|endswith: '.py'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate software using similar naming conventions (rare)
level: high

sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects when processes access the Telegram tdata folder and create a zip archive, which is how Arkanix packages stolen Telegram sessions for exfiltration to its C2 panel.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: Arkanix Stealer Telegram Data Collection
id: rule-0004
status: experimental
description: Detects collection and zipping of Telegram data by Arkanix Stealer
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    CommandLine|contains|all:
      - 'Telegram Desktop\tdata'
      - 'tdata_session.zip'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate Telegram backup tools
level: high

sigma_rules.plain_english: Detects Python processes making network connections to the arkanix.pw domain on API endpoints that register victims, download stealer payloads, or update malware configuration.
sigma_rules.rule:
title: Arkanix Stealer C2 Communication
id: rule-0005
status: experimental
description: Detects network connections to Arkanix Stealer C2 infrastructure
logsource:
  category: network_connection
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Initiated: 'true'
    DestinationHostname|endswith: 'arkanix.pw'
    DestinationPort: 443
    Image|endswith:
      - '\python.exe'
      - '\pythonw.exe'
  condition: selection
falsepositives:
  - None expected
level: critical

snort_rules.plain_english: Detects when victims connect to the Arkanix C2 session creation endpoint that registers newly compromised machines and establishes initial command-and-control communication.
snort_rules.rule: alert tcp $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET $HTTPS_PORTS (msg:"MALWARE Arkanix Stealer C2 Session Registration"; flow:established,to_server; content:"/api/session/create"; http_uri; content:"arkanix.pw"; content:"Host|3a|"; http_header; fast_pattern; classtype:trojan-activity; sid:9200001; rev:1; metadata:impact_flag red, signature_severity major, attack_target Client_Endpoint, malware_family Arkanix;)

snort_rules.plain_english: Detects download of the main Python stealer payload from arkanix.pw which happens after the loader registers the victim and requests the actual credential-stealing module.
snort_rules.rule: alert tcp $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET $HTTPS_PORTS (msg:"MALWARE Arkanix Stealer Payload Download"; flow:established,to_server; content:"/stealer.py"; http_uri; content:"arkanix.pw"; content:"Host|3a|"; http_header; fast_pattern; classtype:trojan-activity; sid:9200002; rev:1; metadata:impact_flag red, signature_severity critical, attack_target Client_Endpoint, malware_family Arkanix;)

snort_rules.plain_english: Detects requests to the Arkanix features API endpoint that dynamically updates the stealer configuration to enable or disable specific credential harvesting modules on infected systems.
snort_rules.rule: alert tcp $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET $HTTPS_PORTS (msg:"MALWARE Arkanix Stealer Configuration Update"; flow:established,to_server; content:"/api/features/"; http_uri; content:"arkanix.pw"; content:"Host|3a|"; http_header; fast_pattern; classtype:trojan-activity; sid:9200003; rev:1; metadata:impact_flag red, signature_severity major, attack_target Client_Endpoint, malware_family Arkanix;)

snort_rules.plain_english: Detects exfiltration of stolen Telegram session data to the Arkanix delivery endpoint where credential archives containing tdata_session.zip are uploaded for threat actor retrieval.
snort_rules.rule: alert tcp $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET $HTTPS_PORTS (msg:"MALWARE Arkanix Stealer Telegram Data Exfiltration"; flow:established,to_server; content:"/delivery"; http_uri; content:"arkanix.pw"; content:"Host|3a|"; http_header; content:"tdata_session.zip"; http_client_body; fast_pattern; classtype:trojan-activity; sid:9200004; rev:1; metadata:impact_flag red, signature_severity critical, attack_target Client_Endpoint, malware_family Arkanix;)

snort_rules.plain_english: Detects download of the Chrome Elevator browser injection module that Arkanix uses to bypass Chrome's App-Bound Encryption and steal cookies and stored credentials directly from browser memory.
snort_rules.rule: alert tcp $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET $HTTPS_PORTS (msg:"MALWARE Arkanix Stealer Chrome Grabber Module Download"; flow:established,to_server; content:"/api/chrome-grabber-template/"; http_uri; content:"arkanix.pw"; content:"Host|3a|"; http_header; fast_pattern; classtype:trojan-activity; sid:9200005; rev:1; metadata:impact_flag red, signature_severity critical, attack_target Client_Endpoint, malware_family Arkanix;)

adversary.name: Arkanix Stealer
adversary.description: Arkanix Stealer is a rapidly evolving commodity infostealer distributed through Discord and underground forums, disguised as legitimate software tools. It targets Chromium-based browsers and cryptocurrency wallets, utilizing Python-based execution and sophisticated browser injection techniques like Chrome Elevator to bypass modern security protections including App-Bound Encryption.

abilities.name: Simulate User Execution of Disguised Payload
abilities.plain_english: Tests whether security awareness training and endpoint controls detect users launching files disguised as legitimate software installers - the initial delivery method Arkanix uses to trick victims into executing the stealer.
abilities.ability_yaml:
- id: arkanix-001
  name: Simulate User Execution of Disguised Payload
  description: Simulates Arkanix Stealer disguised as legitimate software to test user execution controls
  tactic: execution
  technique:
    attack_id: T1204.002
    name: Malicious File
  platforms:
    windows:
      psh:
        command: |
          Write-Host '[SIM] User executing apparent legitimate installer'
          $fakePath = "$env:TEMP\LegitimateApp_Setup.exe"
          echo '[SIM] Arkanix payload disguised as software tool' > $fakePath
          Write-Host '[SIM] Simulated user double-click execution of disguised file'
        cleanup: |
          Remove-Item -Path "$env:TEMP\LegitimateApp_Setup.exe" -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
          Write-Host '[SIM] Cleanup: Removed simulated disguised file'
    linux:
      sh:
        command: |
          echo '[SIM] User executing apparent legitimate installer'
          fake_path="/tmp/LegitimateApp_Setup.bin"
          echo '[SIM] Arkanix payload disguised as software tool' > "$fake_path"
          echo '[SIM] Simulated user execution of disguised file'
        cleanup: |
          rm -f /tmp/LegitimateApp_Setup.bin
          echo '[SIM] Cleanup: Removed simulated disguised file'

abilities.name: Simulate Python-Based Payload Execution
abilities.plain_english: Tests whether EDR detects embedded Python interpreters executing payloads from memory - the technique Arkanix uses with Nuitka-compiled executables to run Python code without requiring Python installation.
abilities.ability_yaml:
- id: arkanix-002
  name: Simulate Python-Based Payload Execution
  description: Simulates Arkanix Stealer unpacking embedded Python environment and executing from memory
  tactic: execution
  technique:
    attack_id: T1059.006
    name: Python
  platforms:
    windows:
      psh:
        command: |
          Write-Host '[SIM] Unpacking embedded Python environment (Nuitka-style)'
          $pythonSim = "$env:TEMP\embedded_python"
          New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path $pythonSim -Force | Out-Null
          Write-Host '[SIM] Executing Python payload directly from memory simulation'
          echo 'print("[SIM] Arkanix stealer payload executing")' > "$pythonSim\payload.py"
          Write-Host '[SIM] Simulated in-memory Python execution complete'
        cleanup: |
          Remove-Item -Path "$env:TEMP\embedded_python" -Recurse -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
          Write-Host '[SIM] Cleanup: Removed simulated Python environment'
    linux:
      sh:
        command: |
          echo '[SIM] Unpacking embedded Python environment (Nuitka-style)'
          python_sim="/tmp/embedded_python"
          mkdir -p "$python_sim"
          echo '[SIM] Executing Python payload directly from memory simulation'
          echo 'print("[SIM] Arkanix stealer payload executing")' > "$python_sim/payload.py"
          echo '[SIM] Simulated in-memory Python execution complete'
        cleanup: |
          rm -rf /tmp/embedded_python
          echo '[SIM] Cleanup: Removed simulated Python environment'

abilities.name: Simulate Process Injection into Browser
abilities.plain_english: Tests whether EDR detects code injection into browser processes - the Chrome Elevator technique Arkanix uses to inject code directly into Chrome to bypass App-Bound Encryption and steal protected credentials.
abilities.ability_yaml:
- id: arkanix-003
  name: Simulate Process Injection into Browser
  description: Simulates Chrome Elevator injecting code into Chrome browser process to bypass App-Bound Encryption
  tactic: defense-evasion
  technique:
    attack_id: T1055
    name: Process Injection
  platforms:
    windows:
      psh:
        command: |
          Write-Host '[SIM] Chrome Elevator: Identifying Chrome browser process'
          $chromeProc = Get-Process chrome -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Select-Object -First 1
          if ($chromeProc) {
            Write-Host "[SIM] Simulating code injection into Chrome PID: $($chromeProc.Id)"
          } else {
            Write-Host '[SIM] Chrome not running - simulating injection into browser process'
          }
          Write-Host '[SIM] Injected code operating inside Chrome authorized environment'
          Write-Host '[SIM] Bypassing App-Bound Encryption (Chrome 127+) simulation'
        cleanup: |
          Write-Host '[SIM] Cleanup: No actual injection occurred - simulation only'
    linux:
      sh:
        command: |
          echo '[SIM] Chrome Elevator: Identifying Chrome browser process'
          chrome_pid=$(pgrep chrome | head -n 1)
          if [ -n "$chrome_pid" ]; then
            echo "[SIM] Simulating code injection into Chrome PID: $chrome_pid"
          else
            echo '[SIM] Chrome not running - simulating injection into browser process'
          fi
          echo '[SIM] Injected code operating inside Chrome authorized environment'
          echo '[SIM] Bypassing security protections simulation'
        cleanup: |
          echo '[SIM] Cleanup: No actual injection occurred - simulation only'

abilities.name: Simulate Browser Credential Harvesting
abilities.plain_english: Tests whether security tools detect access to browser credential stores - the end goal of Arkanix's Chrome Elevator injection where it harvests cookies and stored passwords from Chromium-based browsers including Chrome, Edge, and Opera.
abilities.ability_yaml:
- id: arkanix-004
  name: Simulate Browser Credential Harvesting
  description: Simulates Arkanix Stealer accessing browser credential stores from multiple Chromium browsers
  tactic: credential-access
  technique:
    attack_id: T1555.003
    name: Credentials from Web Browsers
  platforms:
    windows:
      psh:
        command: |
          Write-Host '[SIM] Accessing Chromium-based browser credential stores'
          $browsers = @('Chrome', 'Edge', 'Opera', 'Vivaldi', 'Brave')
          foreach ($browser in $browsers) {
            Write-Host "[SIM] Simulating credential extraction from $browser"
          }
          $simOutput = "$env:TEMP\browser_creds_sim.txt"
          echo '[SIM] Harvested cookies and stored credentials' > $simOutput
          Write-Host '[SIM] Chrome Elevator: Direct access to protected credential stores'
        cleanup: |
          Remove-Item -Path "$env:TEMP\browser_creds_sim.txt" -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
          Write-Host '[SIM] Cleanup: Removed simulated credential harvest output'
    linux:
      sh:
        command: |
          echo '[SIM] Accessing Chromium-based browser credential stores'
          for browser in Chrome Edge Opera Vivaldi Brave; do
            echo "[SIM] Simulating credential extraction from $browser"
          done
          sim_output="/tmp/browser_creds_sim.txt"
          echo '[SIM] Harvested cookies and stored credentials' > "$sim_output"
          echo '[SIM] Direct access to protected credential stores'
        cleanup: |
          rm -f /tmp/browser_creds_sim.txt
          echo '[SIM] Cleanup: Removed simulated credential harvest output'

abilities.name: Simulate Cryptocurrency Wallet Theft
abilities.plain_english: Tests whether security controls detect access to cryptocurrency wallet files and extensions - Arkanix targets wallet data from standalone clients like Electrum and browser extensions like MetaMask and Binance to steal cryptocurrency assets.
abilities.ability_yaml:
- id: arkanix-005
  name: Simulate Cryptocurrency Wallet Theft
  description: Simulates Arkanix Stealer extracting cryptocurrency wallet data from standalone clients and browser extensions
  tactic: credential-access
  technique:
    attack_id: T1552.001
    name: Credentials In Files
  platforms:
    windows:
      psh:
        command: |
          Write-Host '[SIM] Scanning for cryptocurrency wallet data'
          $wallets = @('MetaMask', 'Binance', 'Exodus', 'Electrum', 'Ethereum')
          foreach ($wallet in $wallets) {
            Write-Host "[SIM] Simulating wallet data extraction from $wallet"
          }
          $simWallet = "$env:TEMP\crypto_wallets_sim.txt"
          echo '[SIM] Harvested wallet credentials and keys' > $simWallet
          Write-Host '[SIM] Targeting browser extensions and standalone wallet clients'
        cleanup: |
          Remove-Item -Path "$env:TEMP\crypto_wallets_sim.txt" -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
          Write-Host '[SIM] Cleanup: Removed simulated wallet theft output'
    linux:
      sh:
        command: |
          echo '[SIM] Scanning for cryptocurrency wallet data'
          for wallet in MetaMask Binance Exodus Electrum Ethereum; do
            echo "[SIM] Simulating wallet data extraction from $wallet"
          done
          sim_wallet="/tmp/crypto_wallets_sim.txt"
          echo '[SIM] Harvested wallet credentials and keys' > "$sim_wallet"
          echo '[SIM] Targeting browser extensions and standalone wallet clients'
        cleanup: |
          rm -f /tmp/crypto_wallets_sim.txt
          echo '[SIM] Cleanup: Removed simulated wallet theft output'

campaigns.title: LeakNet ransomware uses ClickFix, Deno runtime in stealthy attacks
campaigns.analyst_summary: LeakNet ransomware operators are deploying their payload through a multi-stage infection chain that begins with malicious links delivering PowerShell scripts (Romeo*.ps1) and VBS files (Juliet*.vbs), followed by DLL sideloading via a malicious jli.dll placed in C:\ProgramData\USOShared and executed through legitimate Java processes. The campaign uses embedded payloads for defense evasion and communicates with C2 infrastructure at okobojirent[.]com. The sophisticated execution chain leverages multiple scripting languages (PowerShell, VBS, JavaScript) and file-based persistence mechanisms to evade detection before deploying the ransomware payload.

iocs.context: PowerShell scripts cleverly named Romeo*.ps1 used to initiate the Deno process
Visual Basic Script cleverly named Juliet*.vbs used to initiate the Deno process
DLL sideloading using jli.dll loaded via Java in C:\ProgramData\USOShared
DLL sideloading location where jli.dll is loaded via Java in C:\ProgramData\USOShared
The campaign's C2 infrastructure leverages both custom domains (e.g., okobojirent[.]com, mshealthmetrics[.]com) and cloud storage.
The campaign's C2 infrastructure leverages both custom domains (e.g., okobojirent[.]com, mshealthmetrics[.]com) and cloud storage.

Darknet leak site operated by LeakNet ransomware group

Staging server for exfiltration used in LeakNet infrastructure (2025)
Dropped executable observed in LeakNet campaigns
Registry modification used for persistence in LeakNet campaigns

In every confirmed LeakNet incident, we observed the same post-exploitation chain: jli.dll side-loading into Java in the USOShared directory, PsExec-based lateral movement, and S3 bucket payload staging.
